elements.sent-2411-003: In distributed optimization, the communication of model updates can be a performance bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-004: Consequently, gradient compression has been proposed as a means of increasing optimization throughput.
elements.sent-2411-005: In general, due to information loss, compression introduces a penalty on the number of iterations needed to reach a solution.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we investigate how the iteration penalty depends on the interaction between compression and problem structure, in the context of non-convex stochastic optimization.
elements.sent-2411-007: We focus on linear compression schemes, where compression and decompression can be modeled as multiplication with a random matrix.
elements.sent-2411-008: We consider several distributions of matrices, among them random orthogonal matrices and matrices with random Gaussian entries.
elements.sent-2411-009: We find that in each case, the impact of compression on convergence can be quantified in terms of the norm of the Hessian of the objective, using a norm defined by the compression scheme.
elements.sent-2411-010: The analysis reveals that in certain cases, compression performance is related to low-rank structure or other spectral properties of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-011: In these cases, our bounds predict that the penalty introduced by compression is significantly reduced compared to worst-case bounds that only consider the compression level, ignoring problem data.
elements.sent-2411-012: We verify the theoretical findings on several optimization problems, including fine-tuning an image classification model.
elements.sent-2411-013: Training machine learning models on large datasets often involves distributed optimization.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this context distributed training means using multiple compute nodes to train a machine learning model collaboratively.
elements.sent-2411-015: The purpose could be to increase the overall throughput of training, and thus reach a solution in a shorter amount of time compared to single-node training, or it could be to increase accuracy, by indirectly combining datasets across nodes.
elements.sent-2411-016: A significant performance bottleneck in distributed training comes from communication among training processes.
elements.sent-2411-017: In algorithms like Parallel SGD, model updates need to be synchronized at every optimization step, and this is typically carried out using a collective communication primitive like all-reduce \cite{distbelief,zinkevich2010,paramserver .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In many HPC settings, there is an imbalance between local compute power and network bandwidth, such that time spent in network communication accounts for the majority of time in each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-019: One approach to address this bottleneck is to reduce communication by compressing model updates.
elements.sent-2411-020: In these schemes, model updates are compressed before communication, and these compressed representations are synchronized through collective communication and then decompressed.
elements.sent-2411-021: When the savings in communication time is greater than the overhead from compression and decompression, the result is faster training iterations.
elements.sent-2411-022: That is, we achieve higher throughput.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, higher throughput alone is not sufficient to achieve a benefit in machine learning problems.
elements.sent-2411-024: Practical compression schemes are lossy, meaning they introduce approximation errors into the training algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-025: This reduces the effectiveness of individual model updates.
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence in order for compression to be useful, the increase in throughput needs to be of sufficient magnitude to make up for the increased number of training steps that may be required to compensate for these approximation errors.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this work we examine the performance of several compression schemes.
elements.sent-2411-028: We analyze how compression impacts the convergence of the algorithms, in terms of the number of iterations required to reach a solution.
elements.sent-2411-029: We focus our attention on three schemes in particular.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the first scheme, referred to as $\randk$, at each step of optimization a random subset of $k$ elements of the model update is synchronized.
elements.sent-2411-031: The second scheme, $\haark$, involves compressing gradients by projection to a random $k$-dimensional subspace, using random matrices distributed according to the Haar distribution on Orthogonal matrices.
elements.sent-2411-032: The third scheme we consider is $\normk$, and involves compression using matrices with i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our optimization objective is to find a local minimum of a function $f: \mathbb{R ^m\to\mathbb{R}$: \begin{equation}\min_{x\in\reals^m}f(x)\end{equation} We allow $f$ to be non-convex, and our analyses bounds the number of iterations needed to find approximate stationary points.}
elements.sent-2411-034: These are values of the parameter $x$ where $\|\nabla f(x)\|^2$ is small.
elements.sent-2411-035: The pseudo-code for the compression approach we study is listed in Algorithm \ref{algo1 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We assume that optimization is carried out by $r$ tasks.
elements.sent-2411-037: Iteration $t$ begins with a stochastic gradient computation at each task, producing the gradient estimates $g_{t ^i$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: These gradients are then compressed locally, using a linear operator $Q_t$ which is assumed to be the same across tasks.
elements.sent-2411-039: These compressed gradients are synchronized via all-reduce, and then decompressed into the vector $\Delta_t$.
elements.sent-2411-040: This $\Delta$ is then combined with a step size $\epsilon_t$ to update the parameter, obtaining $x_{t+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Note that depending on the type of compression used, it is not necessary to actually form the compression matrix $Q_t$ (for instance, when using \randk{ ).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{algorithm}[H] \caption{SGD with compression (runs on $r$ nodes in parallel)\label{algo1}} \fw{\phantom{ }1:}\, input: Initial point $x_1 \in \reals^m$, same for all tasks. \fw{\phantom{ }2:}\, for $t=1,2,\hdots$ do \fw{\phantom{ }3:}\, \quad Compute stochastic gradient $g_t^i$ \fw{\phantom{ }4:}\, \quad Compress: $\Delta_t^i \gets Q^T_tg_t^i$ \fw{\phantom{ }5:}\, \quad All-reduce: $\Delta_t \gets \frac{1}{r}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{r}\Delta_t^i$ \fw{\phantom{ }6:}\, \quad Decompress $h_t \gets Q_t\Delta_t$ \fw{\phantom{ }7:}\, \quad Perform update: $x_{t+1} = x_{t} - \epsilon_t h_{t}$ \end{algorithm}
elements.sent-2411-042: The forms of compression we consider in this work can all be described with linear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-043: That is, in each case the operations of compression and decompression can be expressed as linear maps applied to the original vector $g_t$ or it's compressed representation $\Delta$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-044: No matter which compression method is used, the next iterate $x_{t+1}$ takes the form \begin{equation}\label{q-formula} x_{t+1} = x_{t} - \epsilon Q_{t}Q_{t}^Tg_{t}\end{equation} where $g_{t = \frac{1 {r}\sum\limits_{i=1 ^{r g_{t ^i$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-174: In this section we present some experimental results on least squares and logistic regression problems.
elements.sent-2411-175: The aim of these experiments is to empirically investigate whether the bounds derived above correlate with real-world performance.
elements.sent-2411-176: We find that the experiments confirm the theory above: As a rule, $\randk$ is the worst performer among the three compression schemes, while $\normk$ and $\haark$ have similar performance, with $\haark$ having a slight advantage.
elements.sent-2411-177: In this setting, we consider a linear regression problem.
elements.sent-2411-178: The task is to learn a set of regression coefficients (and a bias term) that map an $n=100$ dimensional vector to a scalar.
elements.sent-2411-179: The number of input/output pairs is $10$ and they are randomly sampled from a Gaussian distribution on $\mathbb{R ^{100+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-180: In these experiments we use Corollary \ref{prop:non-convex-no-noise to compute the step-sizes that minimize the upper bound on performance.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The numerical results are presented in the top row of Figure \ref{fig:results .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Each of the plots shows a comparison of non-compressed and compressed training at various values of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-183: On the top left, we plot the ratio of the (squared) gradient norm returned by compressed and non-compressed training after a fixed number of steps $N=40$.
elements.sent-2411-184: As would be expected due to the lossy nature of compression, non-compressed training finds a parameter with a smaller gradient and the gap between compressed and non-compressed training narrows as the compression parameter $k$ is increased.
elements.sent-2411-185: In this relatively simple case, we can exactly calculate the right-hand side of Corollary \ref{prop:non-convex-no-noise under both compressed and non-compressed training, and obtain an estimate of the relative performance of each compression scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-186: This is indicated by an '$\times$' in the plots, and we see that the predicted performance comparison is within the error bars of performance in each case.
elements.sent-2411-187: On the top right we compare the algorithms by examining the number of iterations needed by compressed and non-compressed optimization to find a point with a small gradient, defined as $\|\nabla f(x)\|^2 < 10^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Specifically, we plot the ratio of the number of iterations needed with and without compression in order to meet this performance criteria.
elements.sent-2411-189: We find that the relative performance of the compression schemes follows a similar pattern to what was observed with the relative gradient norm.
elements.sent-2411-190: For reference, non-compressed optimization required an average of $N=15$ iterations to meet this criteria.
elements.sent-2411-191: In both the left and right plots, the error bars represent one standard deviation of performance, measured by repeating the experiment across $500$ random datasets.

elements.sent-2411-270: We focus on linear schemes, where compression and decompression can be modeled as multiplication with a random matrix.
elements.sent-2411-271: We consider several distributions of matrices, among them Haar-distributed orthogonal matrices and matrices with random Gaussian entries.
